title: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation
description: RAG is the dominant production pattern for grounding LLMs in private or live data. FAANG ML system design interviews frequently ask you to design a RAG pipeline from scratch — including chunking, embedding, retrieval, and re-ranking.

sections.title: RAG Architecture Overview
sections.description:
RAG has two phases: Indexing (offline) and Retrieval + Generation (online).

Indexing: Documents → Chunking → Embedding Model → Vector Store
Query time: User query → Embed query → ANN search → Top-K chunks → LLM with context → Response
sections.codeBlock:
# Minimal RAG pipeline skeleton
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import faiss
import numpy as np

class RAGPipeline:
    def __init__(self, embed_model='all-MiniLM-L6-v2'):
        self.model = SentenceTransformer(embed_model)
        self.index = None
        self.chunks = []

    # INDEXING (offline)
    def index_documents(self, docs: list[str], chunk_size=512):
        self.chunks = self._chunk(docs, chunk_size)
        vecs = self.model.encode(self.chunks, normalize_embeddings=True)
        dim = vecs.shape[1]
        self.index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)  # Inner product on L2-normalized = cosine
        self.index.add(vecs.astype('float32'))

    # RETRIEVAL (online)
    def retrieve(self, query: str, top_k=5) -> list[str]:
        q_vec = self.model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype('float32')
        scores, ids = self.index.search(q_vec, top_k)
        return [self.chunks[i] for i in ids[0] if i != -1]

    def _chunk(self, docs, size):
        # ponytail: naive split; real pipeline uses sentence boundaries
        chunks = []
        for doc in docs:
            words = doc.split()
            chunks += [' '.join(words[i:i+size]) for i in range(0, len(words), size)]
        return chunks

sections.title: Chunking Strategies
sections.description:
How you chunk documents dramatically affects retrieval quality. The chunking strategy is often the first question in a RAG system design interview.

Fixed-size (naive): Split every N tokens. Fast, simple, but may cut sentences mid-thought.
Sentence-boundary chunking: Use spaCy/NLTK to split at sentence boundaries. Better coherence.
Sliding window with overlap: Chunks overlap by K tokens. Ensures context around chunk boundaries isn't lost. Most common in production (e.g., LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter defaults to 200 token overlap).
Semantic chunking: Embed sentences, find breakpoints where embedding similarity drops — chunk at topic boundaries. Higher quality, more compute.
Document-aware: Respect natural structure — markdown headers, PDF sections, code blocks.
sections.codeBlock:
def sliding_window_chunks(text: str, chunk_size=512, overlap=128) -> list[str]:
    """
    Overlapping chunks prevent context loss at boundaries.
    chunk_size and overlap are in tokens (approximated as words here).
    ponytail: real impl uses tiktoken for accurate token counts
    """
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    step = chunk_size - overlap
    for i in range(0, len(words), step):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
        if i + chunk_size >= len(words):
            break
    return chunks

# Semantic chunking (embedding-based breakpoints)
def semantic_chunks(sentences: list[str], model, threshold=0.3):
    vecs = model.encode(sentences, normalize_embeddings=True)
    chunks, current = [], [sentences[0]]
    for i in range(1, len(sentences)):
        sim = float(vecs[i] @ vecs[i-1])  # cosine similarity
        if sim < threshold:               # topic shift detected
            chunks.append(' '.join(current))
            current = []
        current.append(sentences[i])
    if current:
        chunks.append(' '.join(current))
    return chunks

sections.title: Vector Databases & ANN Search
sections.description:
Vector databases index dense embeddings for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. Exact search is O(N×D) — too slow for millions of vectors. ANN trades a small accuracy loss for orders-of-magnitude speedup.

FAISS (Meta): Library (not a service). IndexFlatIP = exact search. IndexIVFPQ = ANN with inverted file + product quantization. Best for research and when you control the infra.
Pinecone: Managed vector DB, serverless. Zero infra. Good for prototyping and startups.
Weaviate: Open-source, supports hybrid search natively (BM25 + vector). Good for enterprise.
pgvector: Vector extension for PostgreSQL. Great if you're already on Postgres — eliminates a separate service.

FAANG interview tip: Always mention the ANN algorithm (HNSW, IVF, PQ) and justify your index type based on dataset size and query latency requirements.
sections.codeBlock:
import faiss
import numpy as np

# Exact search — O(N) per query
def build_flat_index(vecs):
    dim = vecs.shape[1]
    index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(dim)  # exact L2 search
    index.add(vecs)
    return index

# ANN with IVF (Inverted File) — O(sqrt(N)) approx
def build_ivf_index(vecs, n_cells=100):
    dim = vecs.shape[1]
    quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(dim)
    index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantizer, dim, n_cells)
    index.train(vecs)      # must train before adding
    index.add(vecs)
    index.nprobe = 10      # check 10 cells per query (accuracy vs speed)
    return index

# HNSW — best recall/speed tradeoff for production
def build_hnsw_index(vecs, M=32):
    dim = vecs.shape[1]
    index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(dim, M)  # M = connections per node
    index.add(vecs)
    return index

# Usage
n, d = 10_000, 384
vecs = np.random.rand(n, d).astype('float32')
faiss.normalize_L2(vecs)   # normalize for cosine similarity
index = build_hnsw_index(vecs)

query = np.random.rand(1, d).astype('float32')
faiss.normalize_L2(query)
scores, ids = index.search(query, k=5)  # top-5 neighbors

sections.title: Hybrid Search & Re-Ranking
sections.description:
Pure dense retrieval misses exact keyword matches (names, codes, acronyms). Pure BM25 misses semantic similarity. Hybrid search combines both.

BM25: Sparse retrieval based on TF-IDF variant. Great for exact match, keyword-heavy queries.
Hybrid: Run BM25 + dense retrieval separately → merge results with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) or learned weights. Used in production by Elasticsearch, Weaviate, Cohere.

Re-ranking: Top-K retrieved chunks → cross-encoder re-ranker (e.g., Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker) → top-N final context. Cross-encoders process (query, document) jointly → much more accurate than bi-encoders but too slow for full corpus scan.
sections.codeBlock:
# Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) — combines rankings from multiple sources
def reciprocal_rank_fusion(rankings: list[list], k=60) -> list:
    """
    rankings: list of ranked result lists (e.g., [dense_results, bm25_results])
    k: constant (60 is standard) — diminishes impact of very high ranks
    RRF score: sum(1 / (k + rank)) across all rankings
    """
    scores = {}
    for ranking in rankings:
        for rank, doc_id in enumerate(ranking, start=1):
            scores[doc_id] = scores.get(doc_id, 0) + 1.0 / (k + rank)
    return sorted(scores, key=scores.get, reverse=True)

# Cross-encoder re-ranking (Cohere / BGE)
def rerank(query: str, candidates: list[str], top_n=3):
    # Cross-encoder scores (query, candidate) pairs jointly — more accurate
    import cohere
    co = cohere.Client('your-api-key')
    results = co.rerank(
        query=query,
        documents=candidates,
        top_n=top_n,
        model='rerank-english-v3.0'
    )
    return [candidates[r.index] for r in results.results]

sections.title: Advanced RAG: HyDE, Self-Query, Agentic RAG
sections.description:
HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embedding): Generate a hypothetical answer to the query, embed that, then search. Works because the hypothetical answer is in the same embedding space as real documents — often retrieves better than embedding the short query directly.

Self-Query Retrieval: Use an LLM to parse the natural language query into structured filters + semantic query. E.g., 'Show me Python articles from 2024' → filter: {date: {gte: 2024}} + semantic: 'Python'. Supported natively by LangChain.

Agentic RAG: The LLM decides when and how to retrieve — it can issue multiple queries, reason over retrieved content, and retrieve again iteratively (ReAct, Reflexion patterns). More powerful but harder to debug.
sections.codeBlock:
# HyDE — Hypothetical Document Embedding
def hyde_retrieve(query: str, llm, embedder, index, chunks, top_k=5):
    # Step 1: Generate a hypothetical answer
    hyde_prompt = f'Write a detailed paragraph answering: {query}'
    hypothetical_doc = llm.generate(hyde_prompt)

    # Step 2: Embed the hypothetical doc (not the query)
    h_vec = embedder.encode([hypothetical_doc], normalize_embeddings=True)

    # Step 3: Search using hypothetical doc embedding
    _, ids = index.search(h_vec.astype('float32'), top_k)
    return [chunks[i] for i in ids[0]]

# ReAct-style Agentic RAG skeleton
def agentic_rag(question: str, llm, retriever, max_steps=5):
    context, history = [], []
    for step in range(max_steps):
        # LLM decides: SEARCH(query) or ANSWER(text)
        action = llm.act(question, history, context)
        if action.type == 'SEARCH':
            new_docs = retriever.retrieve(action.query)
            context.extend(new_docs)
            history.append(f'Retrieved: {action.query}')
        elif action.type == 'ANSWER':
            return action.text   # done
    return llm.generate(question, context=context)  # fallback

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks (Lewis et al., 2020) — The original RAG paper that introduced the paradigm
  • RAG vs Fine-Tuning: Pipelines, Tradeoffs, and a Case Study on Agriculture — Empirical comparison of RAG and fine-tuning approaches
  • CRAG — Comprehensive RAG Benchmark — Evaluation framework for RAG systems across different domains
  • LlamaIndex — Production framework for building RAG systems
  • LangChain — Most popular framework for building LLM applications with RAG support
  • FAISS (Meta) — Industry-standard library for vector similarity search and ANN

details:
RAG Failure Modes: (1) Bad chunking → relevant info split across chunks, neither retrieved. Fix: semantic chunking + overlap. (2) Query-document mismatch → query is too short/vague. Fix: HyDE or query expansion. (3) Re-ranker bottleneck → slow cross-encoder on 100s of docs. Fix: two-stage retrieval (retrieve 100, re-rank to 5). (4) Context window overflow → too many chunks fed to LLM. Fix: map-reduce over chunks.

Embedding Model Choice: OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (1536d, cheap), text-embedding-3-large (3072d, best quality). Open-source: BGE-M3 (multi-lingual, best open-source as of 2024), all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (fast, 384d, for low-latency). Always evaluate on your domain — MTEB benchmark scores are a starting point, not a guarantee.

Evaluation: RAGAS framework measures: Context Recall (was relevant info retrieved?), Context Precision (was retrieved info relevant?), Answer Faithfulness (does the answer use the context?), Answer Relevancy (does the answer address the question?). All automatically computed with an LLM judge.
